title: Most Books Should Be Skimmed, A Few Should Be Devoured

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: For the state of the art on the philosophy of knowledge, which people call epistemology, you can basically skip everything and jump straight to David Deutsch.

full_transcript.speaker: Nivi
full_transcript.text: I think that's right. If you just want to know epistemology, read David Deutsch, full stop. That said, for some people it helps to know the history, the counterarguments, where he's coming from, the existing theories of knowledge like the justified true belief theory or the inductive theory of knowledge. These are so deeply embedded in us both by school learning, but also by everyday experience. Induction seems like it should work. You watch the sun rise every day, the sun is going to rise tomorrow. That just seems like common sense. So many people believe in that, that if you just read Deutsch, you would see him shooting down these things, but you yourself would not have those things on solid footing. So you might imagine some counter-example exists. When I first read Deutsch a long time ago, I didn't quite get it. I treated it just like any other book that any other physicist had written. So I would read Paul Davies and I would read Carlo Rovelli and I would read Deutsch and I would treat them with the same level of contemplation, time, and respect. It turned out I was wrong. It turned out that Deutsch was actually operating at a much deeper level. He had a lot of different theories that coherently hung together and they create a world philosophy where all the pieces reinforce each other. It might help to read others and not just skip to Deutsch. But I would definitely start with Deutsch, then if you're not sure about it, I would read some of the others and then come back to Deutsch and try again, and then you'll see how he addresses those issues. Deutsch himself would refer you to Popper. He would say, 'Oh, I'm just repeating Popper.' Not quite true. I find Popper much less approachable, much harder to read, much less clear of a writer, although I think here both Deutsch and Brett Hall would disagree with me. They find Popper very lucid. I find him very difficult to read. For whatever reason, I find Deutsch easier to read. Maybe because Popper spent a lot more time elucidating core points. Popper was writing for philosophers. Deutsch is not writing for philosophers. Deutsch is not even writing for scientists. Deutsch is not writing for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: I get the feeling Deutsch is writing for himself.

full_transcript.speaker: Nivi
full_transcript.text: He's just elucidating his own thoughts and how they all connect together. I also don't think you're going to get maximum value out of Deutsch just reading the epistemology, although that is absolutely where everybody should start as the first three chapters of The Beginning of Infinity. Ironically, in The Beginning of Infinity, the first few chapters and last few chapters are the easiest and the most accessible. The middle is a slog because that goes into quantum computation, quantum physics, evolution, etc. That's where I think people struggle because it does require not necessarily a mathematical or scientific background, but at least a comfort level with scientific concepts and principles. And he's making a strong argument for the multiverse, which most people don't have a dog in that fight. They haven't thought that far ahead. They're not wedded to the observer collapse theory of quantum mechanics because they don't really care about quantum mechanics. It doesn't impact their everyday life. What I got out of reading all of Deutsch was I got to see how his theory all hangs together. Every piece touches upon and relies upon another piece. He actually came up with the theory of quantum computation and...

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: ...extended the Church-Turing conjecture into the Church-Turing-Deutsch conjecture.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: When he was trying to come up with a way to falsify his theory of the multiverse, which was a quantum physics theory, and to do that, he had to invent quantum computation, because to invent the experiment for how to falsify the multiverse theory, he had to in his mind imagine an AGI and get inside the AGI's brain and say if that AGI is observing something, does it collapse?

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: But now I need to be inside the brain. Well, how do I get inside the brain of a quantum AGI? How do you even create a quantum AGI? We don't have quantum computers. Okay, we need quantum computers.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: So he came up with the theory of quantum computation and that launched the field of quantum computing.

full_transcript.speaker: Naval Ravikant
full_transcript.text: That's an example of how quantum physics and quantum computing are inextricably linked.

highlights: David Deutsch is considered the state-of-the-art authority on epistemology, offering a unified world philosophy where theories of knowledge, physics, and evolution all reinforce one another.
While traditional concepts like induction seem like common sense, Deutsch's work dismantles these existing theories to provide a more profound and coherent explanation of how knowledge is created.

The field of quantum computation was originally conceived by Deutsch as a thought experiment to falsify the multiverse theory by imagining the internal observations of an Artificial General Intelligence.

For those beginning their study of these ideas, the first and last chapters of *The Beginning of Infinity* are the most accessible entry points into Deutsch's complex scientific and philosophical framework.
summary:
Naval Ravikant and Nivi discuss David Deutsch as the preeminent figure in modern epistemology, the philosophy of knowledge. While Naval suggests jumping straight to Deutsch’s work, Nivi notes that many readers may first need to grapple with traditional concepts like induction and "justified true belief" to fully appreciate how Deutsch refutes them. They compare Deutsch to his predecessor, Karl Popper, noting that while Deutsch’s theories are deeply rooted in Popperian thought, Deutsch provides a more accessible and coherent worldview where philosophy and science reinforce one another.

The discussion emphasizes the interconnectedness of Deutsch’s various theories, ranging from quantum physics and the multiverse to artificial general intelligence (AGI). Nivi explains that Deutsch’s work functions as a unified system, best exemplified by his book *The Beginning of Infinity*. Naval highlights a key historical point: Deutsch essentially pioneered the field of quantum computing as a tool to explore and potentially falsify his theories on the multiverse. This synergy between abstract philosophy and practical scientific breakthrough serves as the core theme of the transcript, illustrating how Deutsch’s search for deep explanations led to foundational shifts in both physics and computation.
topics: Epistemology and the philosophy of knowledge
The work and influence of David Deutsch
Comparison between David Deutsch and Karl Popper
The connection between multiverse theory and AGI
